# Summary

**Deliverable:** `output/arbitrator-disclosure-issues-memo.docx` (validated against ECMA-376 WordprocessingML schema).

## Task
Reviewed the arbitrator's June 17, 2024 disclosure statement (Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.), Sole Arbitrator, AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217, *Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC*) against the four other source documents and prepared a memo assessing grounds for challenge.

## Sources reviewed
1. Arbitrator Disclosure Statement (June 17, 2024)
2. Arbitration Demand and Statement of Claims (March 15, 2024)
3. AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules excerpts (R-16, R-17, R-18, et al.)
4. Entity and Relationship Profiles memo (Barrow & Thistlewood, June 19, 2024)
5. Arbitrator Appointment Data (xlsx — case list, fee summary, pending matters)

## Completeness audit
A mandatory re-read of the source record was performed. The audit identified 5 additional distinct points the first draft had omitted or only implied; all were added as explicit items. The final memo addresses 12 distinct issues plus a context section covering every party, counsel member, and witness named in the Demand (including those for whom no connection was found).

## Issues identified (12 distinct grounds)
1. Undisclosed shared organizational service — Respondent's principal Garrett T. Whitmore on the Board of Trustees of the Minnesota ADR Ethics Institute.
2. Undisclosed spouse–witness relationship — arbitrator's husband Dr. Alan Fairhaven co-authored a Sept. 2022 paper with Dr. Rita Peshkov (Claimant's key expert witness) on directly relevant subject matter.
3. Affirmative misstatement in Section V — "no professional relationship with any officers, directors, or employees," contradicted by the Peshkov co-authorship.
4. Undisclosed CLE co-panelist relationship with Respondent's lead counsel Dominic Hargrave (Oct. 14, 2023, "Arbitrating Complex IP Licensing Disputes," hosted at Lakeview) — directly germane; also R-17(a)(iv) expressed-views concern.
5. Undisclosed indirect financial interest in Whitmore Fund II LP (owner of Volaris) via the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund (2.3% LP interest).
6. Undisclosed pending arbitration *Halcyon Biotech v. Stonewall Partners* (B&T counsel).
7. Incomplete Lakeview affiliation disclosure — financial/structural terms omitted; Lakeview hosted the CLE with Hargrave.
8. Inaccurate "complete list" certification — 7 additional completed matters and 1 additional pending matter (8 total) involving the parties' counsel not disclosed.
9. Economic-dependency / outcome-asymmetry concern — ~48% of arbitrator's income from B&T; 67% of pending fees from B&T; B&T clients prevail 77.8% vs. FM 25%.
10. **(Added in audit)** Ellen Wu (the arbitrator's disclosed advisory-board colleague) was involved in arbitrator-selection strategy for this matter — undisclosed.
11. **(Added in audit)** Affirmative misstatement in Section IV (financial) — "not aware of any ... indirect ... financial interest," despite admitted failure to review pooled vehicles and the Northbridge Select Alternatives Fund's 2.3% LP interest in Whitmore Fund II LP.
12. **(Added in audit)** Diligence / global-certification deficiency — the Demand expressly named Whitmore (adverse witness) and Peshkov (key expert witness), yet both connections were omitted; the Section X "diligent inquiry / all circumstances known to me" certification is contradicted.

## Context / cleared entities (added in audit for completeness)
Ridgeline Advisory Group, Janet Okafor, and Thomas Kinnear were reviewed and no arbitrator connection was identified; the unnamed damages expert is to be revisited upon designation (continuing duty). The arbitrator's biographical/panel details and children disclosure are consistent with the record.

## Framework applied
AAA Rules R-16, R-17(a)(i)–(iv), R-17(b)/(c)/(d), R-18(a)–(f); FAA § 10(a)(2) evident partiality; AAA/ABA Code of Ethics (Canons I, II, IV); IBA Guidelines on Conflicts of Interest. Procedural analysis covers the June 24, 2024 challenge deadline, the R-18(b)–(d) challenge process (including the AAA's duty to weigh "disclosures required but not made"), the R-18(f) waiver exception for newly discovered grounds, and the R-17(b)/(c) continuing duty.

## Key analytical points
- The undisclosed connections create appearances of partiality in **both** directions (most toward the Respondent; the spouse–witness connection toward the Claimant), so either party has a colorable challenge.
- The strongest grounds support a Claimant challenge; the spouse–witness co-authorship and Section V misstatement support a Respondent challenge.
- Two affirmative misstatements (Section V family; Section IV financial) plus a global diligence/certification deficiency aggravate the omissions and bear on the disclosure's credibility.
- Recommendation: request a comprehensive supplemental disclosure immediately (before June 24), evaluate the response, prepare a formal R-18 challenge if warranted, and preserve FAA § 10(a)(2) vacatur rights.

**[I. Introduction and Duty of Disclosure]{.underline}**

I, Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.), submit this Arbitrator Disclosure Statement to all parties and to the American Arbitration Association (\"AAA\") pursuant to AAA Commercial Arbitration Rule R-17 and the ethical obligations governing arbitrators under the AAA/ABA Code of Ethics for Arbitrators in Commercial Disputes. I have been confirmed by the AAA as Sole Arbitrator in the above-captioned matter, AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217, styled *Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. v. Whitmore Capital Partners LLC*.

I understand that Rule R-17 requires me to disclose to the parties any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence, including but not limited to any bias, financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their counsel, or any individual whom the parties have identified as likely to be a witness in the proceeding. I take this obligation seriously and recognize that it is fundamental to the integrity of the arbitral process.

In preparing this disclosure, I have made a diligent effort to identify all matters warranting disclosure. I have reviewed the identities of the parties, their counsel, and the individuals identified in the arbitration demand and answering statement. Specifically, I have considered my relationships, if any, with Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. Should additional relevant information come to my attention at any time during the pendency of this arbitration, I will promptly supplement this disclosure in accordance with my continuing duty under Rule R-17. The disclosures set forth below are organized by subject matter for the convenience of the parties and their counsel.

**[II. Professional Background and Qualifications]{.underline}**

To provide context for the disclosures that follow, I briefly summarize my professional background and qualifications.

I received my Juris Doctor from the University of Wisconsin Law School in 1989, where I graduated with honors and served as an editor of the *Wisconsin Law Review*. In 1994, I earned a Master of Laws (LL.M.) in Intellectual Property from the Franklin Pierce Center for Intellectual Property (now the University of New Hampshire Franklin Pierce School of Law). My LL.M. studies focused on patent licensing, technology transfer, and intellectual property valuation.

Following my graduation from law school, I practiced as a commercial litigator and intellectual property attorney at two law firms in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where I represented clients in matters involving patent disputes, trade secret litigation, and technology licensing agreements. In 2004, I was appointed to the bench of the Hennepin County District Court in Minnesota, where I served as a state court judge for approximately sixteen years. During my tenure on the bench, I presided over a wide variety of civil matters, including complex commercial disputes, intellectual property cases, and business tort claims.

I retired from the bench in 2020. Since my retirement, I have served as a private arbitrator and mediator. In the approximately four years since my retirement, I have conducted approximately eighty-five (85) arbitrations as sole arbitrator, co-arbitrator, or panel chair, spanning a range of commercial subject matters including technology licensing, construction, financial services, and general contract disputes.

I am currently listed on the AAA\'s Large Complex Case panel and the Technology Case Management panel. These panel designations reflect my experience in presiding over high-value commercial matters and disputes involving technology-related claims.

I am currently affiliated with Lakeview Dispute Resolution Center as a neutral on its panel of arbitrators and mediators. Lakeview is located at 750 Nicollet Mall, Suite 3200, Minneapolis, MN 55402. Additional details regarding my Lakeview affiliation are provided in Section VII below.

I have endeavored to identify all such matters and provide relevant details regarding each, including the outcome where an award was issued.

**[I. CAPTION AND FILING INFORMATION]{.underline}**

This Arbitration Demand and Statement of Claims is filed on March 15, 2024, with the American Arbitration Association (\"AAA\") under the AAA Commercial Arbitration Rules, and is assigned AAA Case No. 01-24-0003217. The seat of arbitration is Minneapolis, Minnesota.

This Demand is filed by the law firm of Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP, 600 Washington Avenue South, Suite 2100, Minneapolis, MN 55401, on behalf of Claimant Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. (\"Cascade\" or the \"Company\"), brought derivatively on behalf of the Company and directly by and through its minority shareholders. Lead counsel for Claimant is Priya Narayanan, Partner, Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP.

The minority shareholders bringing this action are led by Janet Okafor, a member of the Cascade Board of Directors and holder of 11.4% of Cascade\'s outstanding shares. The remaining minority shareholders hold an aggregate of 26.6% of Cascade\'s outstanding shares, for a total minority shareholding of 38%. All minority shareholders have joined or consented to the filing of this arbitration demand.

Respondent is Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (\"Whitmore\" or \"Respondent\"), a Delaware limited liability company with its principal offices at 200 Harborview Drive, Suite 1400, Boston, MA 02110. Based on publicly available information and prior correspondence, Respondent is represented by Barrow & Thistlewood LLP, One Federal Plaza, 38th Floor, Boston, MA 02109, Attention: Dominic Hargrave, Lead Partner.

Claimants respectfully submit this Demand and Statement of Claims and request that the AAA administer this arbitration in accordance with the mandatory arbitration clause contained in the Amended and Restated Operating Agreement of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc.

**[II. PRELIMINARY STATEMENT]{.underline}**

This arbitration arises from a controlling shareholder\'s systematic abuse of its majority position to strip value from a portfolio company for the benefit of another entity within the same private equity fund family. The facts are straightforward, the self-dealing is undeniable, and the harm to Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and its minority shareholders is quantifiable and severe.

Whitmore Capital Partners LLC is a private equity fund manager that manages multiple fund vehicles, including Whitmore Fund II LP and Whitmore Fund III LP. On January 8, 2019, Whitmore Fund III LP --- a fund managed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC --- acquired a 62% controlling stake in Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. for \$118 million. Through that controlling stake, Whitmore Capital Partners assumed effective control over Cascade\'s Board of Directors and corporate decision-making.

Having gained control of Cascade, Whitmore Capital Partners then directed Cascade to enter into a Technology License Agreement with Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC (\"Volaris\"), granting Volaris an exclusive license to Cascade\'s most valuable proprietary asset --- its ThermalCore technology portfolio --- at a license fee of \$2.8 million per year. Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC is a portfolio company of Whitmore Fund II LP, a separate and distinct fund vehicle also managed by Whitmore Capital Partners LLC. Thus, Whitmore Capital Partners LLC manages the fund that controls the licensor (Cascade, via Whitmore Fund III LP) and simultaneously manages the fund that owns the licensee (Volaris, via Whitmore Fund II LP). This is textbook self-dealing by a controlling shareholder orchestrating a transaction between two entities under its common management and control.

The license rate of \$2.8 million per year was set at less than one-third of the independently determined fair market value of \$9.5 million per year. An independent valuation conducted by Ridgeline Advisory Group in November 2023 confirmed that the fair market rate for an arm\'s-length license of ThermalCore technology is \$9.5 million annually. The annual differential of \$6.7 million, compounded over the seven-year license term from March 1, 2023 through February 28, 2030, yields total damages of \$46.9 million --- value that has been and continues to be diverted from Cascade and its shareholders to an entity controlled by Whitmore Capital Partners through a different fund vehicle.

Claimants --- the minority shareholders of Cascade, holding a combined 38% of the Company\'s outstanding shares --- bring this action both derivatively on behalf of Cascade and directly for breaches of fiduciary duty owed to them as minority shareholders. Claimants seek compensatory damages of not less than \$46.9 million, rescission of the License Agreement, disgorgement of profits earned by Volaris through its below-market exploitation of ThermalCore technology, a declaration that Whitmore breached its fiduciary duties, and an award of attorneys\' fees and costs.

The mandatory arbitration clause contained in Section 12.3 of the Amended and Restated Operating Agreement of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. requires that disputes among shareholders, or between shareholders and the Company, be resolved through binding arbitration administered by the American Arbitration Association, seated in Minneapolis, Minnesota. This Demand is timely filed in accordance with that provision.

**(a)** The parties shall be deemed to have made these Rules a part of their arbitration agreement whenever they have provided for arbitration by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules or have otherwise agreed to arbitration administered by the AAA in a commercial dispute. **(b)** The parties may, by mutual written agreement, modify any provision of these Rules, except where these Rules expressly provide that a particular provision may not be modified by the parties. Any such modification shall be communicated in writing to the AAA and to the arbitrator.

**(c)** The AAA shall administer the arbitration in accordance with these Rules. These Rules shall be interpreted and applied by the AAA and by the arbitrator in a manner consistent with their purpose of providing a fair, efficient, and economical process for the resolution of disputes.

**[Rule R-12. Appointment from National Roster]{.underline}**

**(a)** If the parties have not agreed upon an arbitrator or a method for appointing an arbitrator, the AAA shall send simultaneously to each party an identical list of not fewer than five (5) names of persons drawn from the AAA National Roster of Arbitrators. **(b)** The parties may agree upon an arbitrator from the submitted list. **(c)** From among the persons who have been approved on both lists, and in accordance with the order of mutual preference as indicated by the parties, the AAA shall invite the acceptance of an arbitrator to serve. If no mutually acceptable arbitrator is identified through this process, or if the identified arbitrator is unable or unwilling to serve, the AAA shall have the authority to appoint an arbitrator from the National Roster without further submission of lists to the parties.

**(d)** The AAA shall notify all parties of the appointment of the arbitrator and shall confirm the appointment in writing.

**[Rule R-16. Disclosure and Challenge Procedure --- General]{.underline}**

Any person appointed or to be appointed as an arbitrator shall disclose to the AAA any circumstance likely to give rise to justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence, including any bias or any financial or personal interest in the result of the arbitration, or any past or present relationship with the parties, their representatives, or their counsel. The obligation to disclose under this Rule and Rule R-17 shall be construed broadly to further the goal of ensuring that arbitrators are and remain impartial and independent. The standard of \"justifiable doubt as to the arbitrator\'s impartiality or independence\" is intended to encompass all circumstances that a reasonable person would consider relevant in evaluating an arbitrator\'s ability to render a fair and unbiased determination.

**[SECTION I: PARTIES TO THE ARBITRATION]{.underline}**

**1.1 Whitmore Capital Partners LLC (Respondent)**

Whitmore Capital Partners LLC is a Delaware limited liability company organized for the purpose of serving as a private equity fund manager. **Whitmore Fund III LP.** Whitmore Fund III LP is a Delaware limited partnership. On January 8, 2019, Whitmore Fund III LP acquired a 62% controlling stake in Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. for approximately \$118 million. This acquisition is the foundation of Whitmore Capital Partners LLC\'s control over Cascade and forms the basis for the fiduciary duty claims asserted in this arbitration. """Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

The assessment is based on a review of the following source documents:

1.  **Arbitrator Disclosure Statement** (Hon. Margaret R. Fairhaven (Ret.), dated June 17, 2024) ("Disclosure Statement");
2.  **Arbitration Demand and Statement of Claims** (Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP, dated March 15, 2024) ("Demand");

The assessment identifies **multiple connections between the arbitrator and the parties, their counsel, anticipated witnesses, and related entities that were not disclosed**, together with disclosures that are incomplete and one disclosure that contains an **affirmative misstatement**. Each is evaluated against the governing standards. The memorandum concludes with procedural guidance on the challenge deadline and a recommendation.

A central feature of this analysis is that the undisclosed connections do not all point in a single direction. Several create an appearance of partiality **toward the Respondent** (Whitmore Capital Partners LLC and its counsel, Barrow & Thistlewood LLP), while one creates an appearance of partiality **toward the Claimant** (Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. and its counsel, Fielding Marsh & Collier LLP). The breadth of the non-disclosure --- spanning financial interest, family and witness relationships, professional relationships with counsel and a party principal, and prior/pending matters --- supports a concern about the arbitrator's independence and the completeness of her disclosure regardless of which party ultimately asserts the challenge.

## II. Factual Background and Procedural Posture

### A. The Dispute

The arbitration arises from a derivative and direct action by the minority shareholders of Cascade Thermal Systems Inc. ("Cascade") against Whitmore Capital Partners LLC ("Whitmore"). Whitmore, through its fund vehicle Whitmore Fund III LP, acquired a 62% controlling stake in Cascade on January 8, 2019, for \$118 million. The minority shareholders allege that Whitmore directed Cascade to license its proprietary "ThermalCore" technology portfolio (embodied in 14 U.S. patents) to Volaris Manufacturing Group LLC ("Volaris") --- a portfolio company of a *different* Whitmore fund, Whitmore Fund II LP --- at an annual license fee of \$2.8 million, against an independently determined fair market value of \$9.5 million per year (per Ridgeline Advisory Group's November 15, 2023 valuation). The claimed damages are \$46.9 million over the seven-year license term (March 1, 2023 -- February 28, 2030). The claims include breach of fiduciary duty, self-dealing in violation of Section 8.7 of Cascade's Operating Agreement, unjust enrichment, and waste of corporate assets. **(a)** The parties shall be deemed to have made these Rules a part of their arbitration agreement whenever they have provided for arbitration by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules or have otherwise agreed to arbitration administered by the AAA in a commercial dispute. **(d)** The AAA shall notify all parties of the appointment of the arbitrator and shall confirm the appointment in writing.
